value: This review covers the physical metallurgy, processing strategies, strengthening mechanisms and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels for automotive applications, and suggests future avenues.
Current state of Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steels
Single-phase gamma after solution treatment; precipitation of kappa'-carbide when Al>7% and C>0.7%. Best properties in Al 7-10%, Mn 15-30%, C 0.7-1.2%.
Austenite based duplex low density steel
Hot-rolled: gamma + small fraction of delta-ferrite (<50%); often contains intragranular nano-sized kappa'-carbides and delta-ferrite stringers.
Used as baseline in various figures to compare the effect of Al addition.
Matrix is delta-ferrite; Al content above 7% leads to embrittlement due to ordering (K1, B2, DO3).
Ferrite based duplex low density steel
Hot-rolled microstructure: gamma + delta-ferrite, with delta-ferrite fraction >50%. Cold rolling and intercritical annealing produce TRIP/TWIP effects.
Solution treatment at 900-1100 °C in single-phase gamma region, water quenching; optional aging at 450-700 °C for kappa' precipitation hardening, or cold rolling + recrystallization annealing.
Age hardening from intragranular kappa'-carbides; overaging leads to grain boundary kappa* and loss of ductility.
Hot rolling in single gamma or (gamma+delta) region, solution treatment 900-1100 °C, water quenching; optional aging at 450-700 °C.
Process variants to control kappa-carbide precipitation and delta-ferrite morphology.
Solution treatment at 1100 °C, water quenching (reference condition for TWIP).
Ingot casting, homogenization at 1100-1250 °C, hot rolling, cold rolling, recrystallization annealing at 700-950 °C.
Grain refinement limited; texture control via cold rolling and annealing.
Hot rolling in (delta+gamma) region, cold rolling, intercritical annealing at 700-900 °C followed by fast cooling or austempering.
Austempering used to stabilize austenite. Control of austenite grain size and stability is critical for TRIP/TWIP.
For non-age hardenable grades; grain refinement and ductility restoration.
Precipitation of intragranular kappa'-carbides; longer times cause kappa* and alpha (B2/DO3).
Grain size 40-90 µm after annealing; texture random with Cube fibre.
Controls volume fraction, grain size and stability of austenite.
depending on Al and C content
Single-phase austenite with varying density of SRO / kappa' precipitates depending on aging
Austenite matrix with delta-ferrite stringers and intragranular kappa' carbides
Elongated delta-ferrite bands with possible grain boundary kappa-carbides
Banded mixture of coarse delta-ferrite and refined austenite (or transformation products)
Shearing of kappa' carbides by dislocations enhances planar slip and reduces work hardening rate.
Planar glide dominates; slip bands refine dynamically (DSBR) or form microbands (MBIP) depending on SRO/precipitates.
Aging: SRO -> kappa' (L'12) -> kappa (E21); overaging: grain boundary kappa* and b-Mn.
initially coherent (K-S OR), loses coherency upon coarsening
Strain partitioning occurs between gamma and delta; gamma exhibits higher strain hardenability.
During aging, kappa' coarsens and may lose coherency; grain boundary kappa* and alpha (B2/DO3) may form.
incoherent or semicoherent, crack initiation site
far inferior to 304 stainless steel, comparable to conventional high strength steels
200 J/cm2 (CVN) at RT in solution-treated condition; much reduced after aging
160-175 GPa for Fe-20Mn-(7-10)Al-(0.7-1.2)C cold rolled and annealed; a 2% reduction per 1% Al addition
specific energy absorption ~0.5 J/mm3, similar to Mn TWIP steels and 50% higher than DC04/HSLA steels
Revealed SRO, kappa' carbides, planar glide, deformation twins, ordered phases.
Microstructural characterization of phases, precipitates, dislocations, and deformation mechanisms.
OM, SEM, TEM, XRD, ECCI, APT
Yield strength 0.4-1.0 GPa, UTS 0.6-2.0 GPa, elongation 30-100% in austenitic steels; strain hardening dominated by planar glide and SRO/kappa' interaction.
Assess strength, elongation, strain hardening behaviour.
Estimate density of ferritic and austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys
Composition boundary for intragranular kappa' precipitation
Based on critical lattice constant of austenite ~0.3670 nm.
Reasonable agreement but deviations at high Mn and Al; e.g., calculated formation temperatures of kappa and M23C6 underestimated by up to 150 °C.
Young's modulus of low density steels decreases by 2-2.5% per 1 wt% Al addition and is significantly lower than that of conventional HSLA steels (~160-175 GPa vs 210 GPa).
Addition of Al suppresses cementite precipitation and promotes short-range ordering (SRO) and kappa'-carbide, which leads to glide plane softening and planar slip.

Three new deformation mechanisms are introduced for high-SFE austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels: microband induced plasticity (MBIP), dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR), and shear band induced plasticity (SIP).
Effective Al composition range for austenitic low-density steels with best mechanical properties is 7-10 wt% Al, with typical compositions: 15-30% Mn, 7-10% Al, 0.7-1.2% C.
The ferrite based duplex low-density steels can achieve a combination of TRIP and TWIP effects, showing UTS up to 900 MPa and total elongation up to 70%.
Specific stiffness of low density steels (E^(n)/rho) is not higher than that of HSLA steels and aluminium alloys; therefore, parts must be selected where stiffness is not the limiting factor.
Fatigue behaviour data are limited and conflicting; formability, weldability, and coatability are not well documented.
Corrosion resistance of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys is not improved over conventional high strength steels; coating is needed for automotive applications.
